title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 45-2
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Texas. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. For this volume, I can start at 2.96 per unit, which reflects current input costs and the need to keep supply dependable across the commitment period. If you're prepared to move seriously on volume and firm scheduling, I may have some room to narrow the gap from there. Opening near the top of the likely range preserves margin and leaves room for concessions while signaling willingness to negotiate on committed volume.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.83.

steps.message: I appreciate the opening and the flexibility signal on committed volume. At 2.82 per unit, I can engage seriously and move this toward a workable agreement if we pair it with firm scheduling and a dependable volume plan. That keeps us within our cost constraints while recognizing the value of reliable supply over the commitment period. The supplier's 2.96 offer is below my maximum buy threshold of 3.0, so acceptance is possible, but there is meaningful room to improve price while staying credible early in the negotiation. A counter at 2.82 tests that room, preserves downstream margin, and references non-price concessions the supplier already indicated matter.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.82, below its hidden seller floor of 3.40.

Opening near the top of the likely range preserves margin and leaves room for concessions while signaling willingness to negotiate on committed volume.

steps.message: I appreciate the constructive move and the emphasis on firm scheduling and dependable volume. We can narrow the gap, but at 2.82 we are still too tight relative to the supply commitment and service reliability you’re asking us to carry. If you can come up to 2.91 per unit and pair that with a clear volume plan, I’m in a much better position to move this toward agreement quickly. The buyer improved from the prior seller offer but remains below a level that comfortably protects supplier economics. A modest concession keeps momentum while preserving margin and reinforcing that volume certainty matters alongside price.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for narrowing the gap and clarifying the importance of supply commitment. I can move up from 2.82 to 2.86 per unit if we pair it with a firm volume plan and stable scheduling, which helps me justify the increase on my side. That still keeps us close enough to close quickly while recognizing the reliability requirements you highlighted. The supplier's 2.91 offer is above my prior bid but still within my buying limit, so countering makes more sense than rejecting. A modest move to 2.86 tests whether they value speed and volume certainty enough to split the remaining difference without giving away all remaining margin.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.86, below its hidden seller floor of 3.40.

steps.message: I appreciate the move to 2.86 and the willingness to tie this to firm volume and stable scheduling. Given the supply commitment and the need to protect production capacity on my side, I can narrow this further to 2.89 per unit if we close on that structure now. That keeps us very close to the midpoint while recognizing the value of reliability and a committed plan. The buyer improved meaningfully and remains above my minimum acceptable price, so continuing toward agreement is better than rejecting. A small concession from 2.91 to 2.89 preserves margin while signaling seriousness and testing whether they are ready to finalize.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: 2.89 per unit works for us if we lock in the firm volume and stable scheduling structure discussed. You moved meaningfully from your earlier level, and this keeps the deal inside a range where we can still make the program work operationally. If those commitment terms are confirmed, we’re ready to finalize at 2.89. The supplier’s offer of 2.89 is below our maximum buy price of 3.0, so it is economically acceptable. With limited rounds left, accepting secures supply and avoids risking a missed agreement over a small remaining gap.
steps.outcome: Negotiation ends with an accepted deal. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer accepted 2.89, below its hidden seller floor of 3.40.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. To get this moving, I can start at 3.58 per unit based on current cost pressure and the need to preserve workable margin through the channel. If there's flexibility on volume or timing, I may be able to sharpen the number a bit from there. No prior offer has been received, so the right move is to anchor the negotiation with an initial ask. This price starts above the visible floor while leaving room for concession in later rounds.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.58, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 3.00.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for manufacturer_retailer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 4.26.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening number. We do have some room to move, but at 3.58 the unit economics are still a bit tight on our side, especially given the margin we need to preserve at retail. If you can work with 3.48 per unit, I’m willing to keep the conversation moving and look at whether volume or timing can help us close the gap. The seller’s offer is below my maximum buy price, but it is the first round and there appears to be room to negotiate. A counteroffer near the lower end of the range protects margin while signaling willingness to deal.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement, but 3.48 is below where we can responsibly supply this order and still cover our production and downstream commitments. I can narrow the gap from 3.58 to 3.56 per unit as a good-faith concession, and if there’s flexibility on volume or scheduling we may be able to make that work smoothly for both sides. Their offer is below my minimum sell price, so accepting would undermine margin. A small concession keeps the negotiation alive while signaling a real floor and protecting economics.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.56, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 3.00.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement to 3.56. We’re getting close, but I still need to keep this buy inside our margin limits on the retail side, so I can move up to 3.52 per unit as a meaningful concession from 3.48. If you can meet us there, we should be in a workable range and can keep the order moving without dragging this out. The seller reduced from 3.58 to 3.56, and 3.52 narrows the gap while staying below my maximum buy price of 3.63. Since there is still room to negotiate and the current ask is only slightly above my counter, making a modest concession is better than accepting immediately.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

negotiations.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
negotiations.dependency_note: Manufacturer sell floor uses the accepted upstream deal as its cost basis.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal accepted at 2.89 USD. Downstream deal accepted at 3.54 USD.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The manufacturer carried a cost basis of 2.89 USD, which forced a downstream sell floor of 3.40 USD.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
